source_intake_record.raw_question_text: The biggest stuffed animal in the world is a snake \( 420 \text{ m} \) long, constructed by Norwegian children. Suppose the snake is laid out in a park as shown in figure, forming two straight sides of a \( 105^\circ \) angle, with one side \( 240 \text{ m} \) long. Olaf and Inge run a race they invent. Inge runs directly from the tail of the snake to its head, and Olaf starts from the same place at the same moment but runs along the snake. If Inge runs the race again at a constant speed of \( 12.0 \text{ km/h} \), at what constant speed must Olaf run to reach the end of the snake at the same time as Inge

image_dependency: question_text explicitly says 'as shown in the figure'
the setup involves geometric layout and spatial relation of two straight sides meeting at a 105° angle
although several numerical facts are stated in text, the problem framing still relies on the diagrammed configuration
multi_step: solver must infer the second snake side length from total length 420 m and given side 240 m
solver must use the 105° included angle to find the direct tail-to-head distance
solver must convert Inge's constant speed and direct distance into a travel time
solver must then compute Olaf's required speed along the full snake length so both finish simultaneously
verifiability: answer_type is short_text and the expected answer is a specific numeric value with units
multiple-choice options are provided, giving an extra consistency check
the reasoning path is standard and checkable with geometric and rate calculations

decision_reasons: question, image, and answer are all present, so the core semantics are fully supported
asset registry passed with no recorded issues or risk flags
initial scoring is strong: high image dependency, multi-step reasoning value, and very high verifiability
the expected answer is a specific short numeric result, which makes the sample easy to validate
